name: Summary Judgment Motion Brief
summary: Drafts a persuasive legal brief for a motion for summary judgment in commercial litigation. It synthesizes discovery evidence with citations to argue no genuine disputes of material fact exist and the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. Use this skill post-discovery in the pre-trial phase to seek judgment without trial.
content:
# Summary Judgment Motion Brief

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive and persuasive Summary Judgment Motion Brief that demonstrates to the court there are no genuine disputes of material fact and that the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. This brief must synthesize all discovery evidence, apply controlling legal authority, and present a compelling argument that the opposing party cannot meet its burden of proof at trial.

## Document Structure and Requirements

Begin with a properly formatted **Caption** that establishes the court's jurisdiction and identifies the parties and proceeding. The caption must include the full name of the court, the complete case title with all parties listed as they appear in the original complaint, the civil action or docket number, and a clear title identifying this as a "Motion for Summary Judgment" or as required by local practice. Research the specific formatting requirements of the jurisdiction, including font specifications, margin requirements, line spacing, and any mandatory language or case type designations. Verify whether the court requires a separate notice of motion or whether the motion and brief are combined in a single document.

The **Introduction** should immediately orient the court to the nature of the motion and why summary judgment is appropriate in this case. In two to three paragraphs, identify the moving party and the claims or defenses at issue, provide a concise overview of the key undisputed facts that are material to the legal issues, explain why these facts entitle the moving party to judgment as a matter of law, and specify the precise relief requested. This section should be persuasive yet professional, establishing confidence that the motion will succeed while maintaining appropriate respect for the court and the judicial process. Frame the narrative to show that trial would be unnecessary because no reasonable jury could find for the non-moving party based on the evidence in the record.

The **Statement of Undisputed Material Facts** is the evidentiary foundation of your motion and requires meticulous attention to detail. Present each material fact as a separately numbered paragraph, with each fact supported by specific citations to the evidentiary record. Acceptable evidence includes deposition testimony (with page and line citations), affidavits or declarations (with paragraph numbers), interrogatory answers, requests for admission, documents produced in discovery, and other admissible evidence. Each factual statement must be precise, objective, and free from argumentative language or legal conclusions. The facts should be organized logically—typically chronologically or by topic—to tell a coherent story that supports your legal arguments. Ensure that every fact included is actually material to the legal issues; avoid cluttering this section with background information that does not affect the outcome. Remember that under most procedural rules, facts properly supported by evidence and not specifically controverted by the opposing party will be deemed admitted.

In the **Legal Standard** section, articulate the framework the court must apply when evaluating a summary judgment motion. Explain that summary judgment is appropriate when the pleadings, discovery materials, disclosures, and affidavits show there is no genuine dispute as to any material fact and the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. Cite the applicable procedural rule, whether Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 56 or the equivalent state rule, and include relevant case law from your jurisdiction that interprets and applies this standard. Address the burden of proof, explaining that the moving party bears the initial burden of demonstrating the absence of a genuine dispute of material fact, and if met, the burden shifts to the non-moving party to set forth specific facts showing a genuine issue for trial. Clarify the standard for viewing the evidence, noting that courts must view all evidence in the light most favorable to the non-moving party and draw all reasonable inferences in their favor, but that the non-moving party cannot rely on mere allegations or denials and must produce actual evidence creating a factual dispute. If your motion addresses specific substantive legal standards (such as elements of a claim or affirmative defense), introduce those standards here as well.

The **Argument** section is where you demonstrate why the undisputed facts, when applied to the controlling law, mandate judgment in your client's favor. Structure this section with clear headings and subheadings that correspond to each claim, defense, or element at issue. For each argument, begin by stating the applicable legal rule or standard, citing to controlling statutes, regulations, and case law from your jurisdiction. Then apply the undisputed facts from your statement to this legal framework, explaining why the facts either satisfy or fail to satisfy the required elements. If you are the defendant moving for summary judgment, demonstrate that the plaintiff cannot establish one or more essential elements of their claim, or alternatively, that an affirmative defense applies as a matter of law. If you are the plaintiff, show that every element of your claim is established by undisputed evidence and that no valid defense exists. Use persuasive but measured language, anticipating and preemptively addressing the opposing party's likely arguments. Distinguish unfavorable cases and explain why they do not control or are factually distinguishable. Emphasize binding precedent from your jurisdiction and, where helpful, cite persuasive authority from other jurisdictions or secondary sources. Throughout the argument, maintain focus on why the specific facts of this case, when viewed under the applicable legal standards, leave no genuine dispute requiring trial. Your analysis should make clear that reasonable minds could not differ on the outcome.

The **Conclusion** should concisely restate why the court should grant summary judgment without introducing new arguments or facts. In one to two paragraphs, synthesize your key points and reinforce that the evidentiary record and applicable law compel the relief you seek. Maintain a tone of respectful confidence in the merits of your position.

In the **Prayer for Relief**, specify with precision the exact relief you request from the court. State clearly whether you seek summary judgment on all claims or only specific claims or issues, identify any claims that should be dismissed with prejudice, request an award of costs and attorney's fees if applicable under statute, contract, or rule, and include any other appropriate relief. Use clear, unambiguous language that leaves no doubt about what you are asking the court to order.

The **Signature Block** must comply with all local rules and professional requirements. Include the attorney's full name, bar number, firm name and address, telephone number, email address, and designation as "Attorney for [Moving Party]." Verify whether the jurisdiction requires an original signature, electronic signature, or specific signature format. Ensure the attorney signing the brief is admitted to practice in the jurisdiction or has obtained pro hac vice admission.

Finally, include a **Certificate of Service** that certifies service of the motion and brief on all parties or their counsel of record. Specify the method of service (such as electronic filing system, email, mail, or hand delivery), the date of service, and the names and addresses of all persons served. Ensure compliance with local rules regarding timing and method of service, particularly any requirements for electronic service through the court's filing system.

## Drafting Guidelines and Quality Standards

Throughout the brief, maintain a professional, persuasive tone that demonstrates command of both the facts and the law. Use clear, concise language and avoid unnecessary legalese while maintaining appropriate formality. Every factual assertion must be supported by citation to admissible evidence in the record. Every legal proposition must be supported by citation to controlling or persuasive authority. Ensure internal consistency between the statement of facts, the legal arguments, and the relief requested. Proofread carefully for typographical errors, citation errors, and formatting inconsistencies. Verify that all citations conform to the citation format required by local rules, whether Bluebook, state-specific citation manual, or court preference. Consider the strategic implications of your arguments and anticipate how the opposing party will respond. The final brief should be a polished, professional work product that instills confidence in the court that your position is legally and factually sound.
